title: East Asia
subtitle: China, Japan, Korea — from terracotta armies to woodblock waves.
intro: East Asian art has a long memory. Forms travel — from Chinese brushwork to Japanese ukiyo-e, from Tang Buddhism to Korean celadon — and each reinvention carries the earlier one inside it.

works.title: Terracotta Army
works.meta: Qin dynasty · 210 BCE · Xi'an, China
works.desc: Over 8,000 soldiers, each with a different face, buried with the first emperor of China to guard him after death. Farmers digging a well found them in 1974.

works.title: Great Wall of China
works.meta: Ming (current form) · 14th–17th c.
works.desc: Not one wall but many, rebuilt across dynasties. The Ming sections visitors see today are a fraction of a system that once stretched over 13,000 miles.

works.title: Mogao Caves (Dunhuang)
works.meta: Silk Road Buddhist · 4th–14th c. · Gansu
works.desc: 492 caves carved into cliff face, filled with murals and gilded clay Buddhas. One sealed chamber held 50,000 manuscripts — the largest trove of its kind.

works.title: Hōryū-ji
works.meta: Asuka period · c. 607 CE · Nara, Japan
works.desc: Among the oldest wooden buildings still standing anywhere in the world. Built when Buddhism had only recently reached Japan.

works.title: Goryeo Celadon
works.meta: Goryeo dynasty · 10th–14th c. · Korea
works.desc: Stoneware with a jade-green glaze so admired that Song-dynasty Chinese called Korean celadon the "first under heaven."

works.title: Along the River During Qingming
works.meta: Northern Song · Zhang Zeduan · 12th c.
works.desc: A five-meter handscroll showing hundreds of people in the Song capital on a spring festival day. Read right to left — it unfolds as you walk past.

works.title: Forbidden City
works.meta: Ming & Qing · 1406–1420 onward · Beijing
works.desc: 980 buildings on 180 acres. For five centuries, commoners who entered uninvited were executed. It is now the world's most-visited museum.

works.title: Kinkaku-ji (Golden Pavilion)
works.meta: Muromachi · 1397, rebuilt 1955 · Kyoto
works.desc: A retirement villa turned Zen temple, its top two floors wrapped in gold leaf. Burned down by a young monk in 1950 — rebuilt in facsimile.

works.title: Ryōan-ji Rock Garden
works.meta: Muromachi · late 15th c. · Kyoto
works.desc: Fifteen stones in raked gravel. From no single vantage can you see all fifteen at once — you're always one short of a complete view.

works.title: Himeji Castle
works.meta: Edo period · 1609 · Hyōgo, Japan
works.desc: The finest surviving Japanese castle, nicknamed "White Heron" for its white plaster. Its winding approach was designed to confuse besiegers.

works.title: The Great Wave off Kanagawa
works.meta: Edo Japan · Hokusai · c. 1831
works.desc: A woodblock print sold for the price of a bowl of noodles. Mount Fuji sits small and steady under the claw of the wave. Hokusai was in his seventies.

works.title: Longmen Grottoes
works.meta: Northern Wei → Tang · 493 CE onward · Luoyang
works.desc: Over 100,000 Buddhas carved into limestone cliffs along the Yi River. The largest is fifty-six feet tall and smiles.

works.title: Yungang Grottoes
works.meta: Northern Wei · 460–525 CE · Datong
works.desc: Fifty-three caves with 51,000 statues. The earliest cliff Buddhas in China — faces still showing Indian and Central Asian lineage.

works.title: Dazu Rock Carvings
works.meta: Tang to Song · 9th–13th c. · Sichuan
works.desc: Buddhist, Daoist, and Confucian figures carved side by side — a rare moment of syncretism preserved in stone.

works.title: Crown of Silla
works.meta: Silla kingdom · 5th–6th c. · Korea
works.desc: Thin gold sheet cut into tree and antler forms, hung with comma-shaped jades and gold spangles. Too fragile to wear — meant for ritual and burial.

works.title: Early Spring
works.meta: Northern Song · Guo Xi · 1072 · China
works.desc: A mountain landscape in ink and subtle color. Guo Xi said a painter should look at nature "as if going on a journey" — this scroll is that walk.

works.title: Potala Palace
works.meta: Tibetan · 1645 onward · Lhasa
works.desc: Thirteen stories of winter residence for the Dalai Lamas — a thousand rooms, ten thousand shrines, two hundred thousand statues. Built where a 7th-century fort once stood.

works.title: Great Buddha of Kamakura
works.meta: Kamakura period · 1252 · Japan
works.desc: A bronze Amida Buddha originally housed in a great hall — but tsunamis washed the building away in 1498 and left the statue to sit in the open ever since.

works.title: Tōdai-ji
works.meta: Nara period · 752 · Japan
works.desc: For a thousand years the largest wooden building in the world. Inside, a 50-foot bronze Buddha, cast in eight pieces, seated in a lotus.

works.title: Byōdō-in
works.meta: Late Heian · 1053 · Uji
works.desc: The Phoenix Hall — bird-shaped pavilion reflecting in its lotus pond. Built as a noble's villa; converted to a temple when he took the tonsure.

works.title: Sanjūsangen-dō
works.meta: Kamakura period · 1266 · Kyoto
works.desc: A 390-foot hall holding 1,001 gilded statues of the thousand-armed Kannon. Each face is different; pilgrims look for their own.

works.title: Nikkō Tōshō-gū
works.meta: Early Edo · 1617 · Japan
works.desc: The mausoleum of Tokugawa Ieyasu, built with the lavish polychrome carving the Tokugawa banned elsewhere. The three wise monkeys are here, above a stable.

works.title: Summer Palace
works.meta: Qing dynasty · 1750 onward · Beijing
works.desc: Three square kilometres of gardens and pavilions around Kunming Lake. Sacked by Anglo-French forces in 1860, looted again by Boxer-era troops in 1900.

works.title: Chinese Ritual Bronzes
works.meta: Shang–Zhou · 16th–3rd c. BCE
works.desc: Tripod cauldrons, wine vessels, and axes cast for ancestor worship. Each is wrapped in a monster mask (taotie) staring from every surface.

works.title: Blue and White Porcelain
works.meta: Yuan → Qing · 14th–19th c. · Jingdezhen
works.desc: Cobalt under a clear glaze, fired to translucence. China's most-exported art form — copied in Iran, Turkey, Netherlands, and eventually the world.

works.title: Prints of Kitagawa Utamaro
works.meta: Edo Japan · 1790s
works.desc: Utamaro specialized in the bijin-ga — studies of beautiful women — often just head and shoulders, in close-up. Scandalously intimate for the time.

works.title: Netsuke
works.meta: Edo Japan · 17th–19th c.
works.desc: Miniature ivory or wood toggles that held purses to kimono sashes. Frogs, rats, mythic beasts — detailed enough to demand a magnifying glass.

works.title: Admonitions Scroll
works.meta: Six Dynasties · Gu Kaizhi (copy) · c. 380 CE
works.desc: A handscroll of court ladies illustrating a Confucian poem of proper conduct. Probably the oldest surviving Chinese narrative painting, preserved in a Tang-era copy.

works.title: Sanxingdui Bronze Masks
works.meta: Sanxingdui culture · c. 1200 BCE · Sichuan
works.desc: Hollow bronze and gold masks with protruding eyes and ears that fan outward. An entirely unknown civilization contemporary with Shang, found in two sacrificial pits in 1986.

works.title: Temple of Heaven
works.meta: Ming · 1420 · Beijing
works.desc: A round blue-roofed temple on a square base — heaven over earth. Every winter solstice for five centuries, the emperor prayed here for the harvest.

works.title: Kōfuku-ji
works.meta: Nara period onward · 710 · Japan
works.desc: One of Nara's four great monasteries. Its treasure hall holds the Asura statue — a three-faced, six-armed youth with an achingly thoughtful face.

works.title: Seokguram Grotto
works.meta: Unified Silla · 774 · Korea
works.desc: A domed granite chamber holding a single seated Buddha, two and a half meters tall. The walls are carved with bodhisattvas and disciples — a sculptural mandala.

works.title: Night-Shining White
works.meta: Tang dynasty · Han Gan · c. 750 · China
works.desc: A tethered stallion struggling at the post — snorting, hooves kicking. The emperor Xuanzong's favorite horse, painted in ink so alive it almost leaves the silk.

works.title: Red and White Plum Blossoms
works.meta: Edo Japan · Ogata Kōrin · early 18th c.
works.desc: A pair of folding screens: two plum trees, red and white, lean toward a stylized silver river of whorls. Rinpa-school decorative painting at its most distilled.

works.title: Fine Wind, Clear Morning (Red Fuji)
works.meta: Edo Japan · Hokusai · c. 1830–1832
works.desc: Hokusai's other famous Fuji — a single triangular form lit crimson by morning sun, clouds streaming off the summit. From the same series as the Great Wave.

works.title: Cypress Trees
works.meta: Momoyama Japan · Kanō Eitoku · 1590
works.desc: An eight-panel folding screen with a single enormous cypress sprawling across gold leaf. The Kano school's monumental court style at its peak.

works.title: Actor Prints of Sharaku
works.meta: Edo Japan · Tōshūsai Sharaku · 1794–1795
works.desc: Kabuki actors caught mid-gesture, their exaggerated faces rendered in crisp black outline against mica backgrounds. Sharaku worked for ten months and then vanished.

works.title: Haniwa
works.meta: Kofun period · 3rd–6th c. CE · Japan
works.desc: Hollow terracotta cylinders and figures set in rings around imperial tumuli. Armored warriors, horses, dancers, shrine maidens — the only accessible record of pre-literate Japanese dress.

works.title: Kakiemon Porcelain
works.meta: Edo Japan · 17th c. onward · Arita
works.desc: Overglaze enamels on a milk-white ground. The Kakiemon workshop at Arita invented a palette — iron red, green, yellow, blue — that Meissen and Chantilly spent decades trying to copy.

works.title: Katsura Imperial Villa
works.meta: Edo Japan · 1620 onward · Kyoto
works.desc: A villa and garden considered one of the finest masterpieces of Japanese architecture. Shoin-zukuri precision: every plank, every sight-line from every veranda, deliberately aligned.

works.title: Leshan Giant Buddha
works.meta: Tang China · 713–803 · Sichuan
works.desc: A seated Maitreya carved directly into a sandstone cliff at the confluence of three rivers. Seventy-one meters tall; his ears alone are seven meters. Built, a monk said, to calm the waters.

works.title: Bulguksa
works.meta: Unified Silla · 751 CE · Gyeongju, Korea
works.desc: A Buddhist temple with twin pagodas (Dabotap and Seokgatap) that encode two sutras in stone form. Burned by the Japanese in 1593, rebuilt in the 1970s on the original foundations.

works.title: Lantingji Xu (Preface to the Orchid Pavilion)
works.meta: Eastern Jin · Wang Xizhi · 353 CE · China
works.desc: A drunkenly perfect calligraphic masterpiece, 324 characters describing a riverside gathering of poets. Wang Xizhi tried to rewrite it next day and could not match it. Later buried with an emperor.

works.title: Dream of the Red Chamber
works.meta: Qing China · Cao Xueqin · 18th c.
works.desc: A classic Chinese novel in 120 chapters, commonly illustrated from Qing to today — the Jia family's decline rendered in garden pavilions, dying flowers, a jade inscribed with fate.

works.title: Shigaraki Ware
works.meta: Muromachi onward · 13th c. · Shiga
works.desc: Earthy brown stoneware with feldspar eruptions on the surface. Prized for tea — the accidental "fire colors" and ash deposits are the whole point.

works.title: Travelers Among Mountains and Streams
works.meta: Northern Song · Fan Kuan · c. 1000 CE
works.desc: A seven-foot hanging scroll — a sheer cliff dominates two-thirds of the picture. Tiny travelers and mules cross the foreground, dwarfed. Widely considered the greatest Chinese landscape painting.

works.title: Jōmon Flame Pottery
works.meta: Jōmon · c. 3000 BCE · Japan
works.desc: Clay pots with rims twisted into exuberant "flame" shapes. Made by prehistoric hunter-gatherers — and, at 15,000 years old, among the oldest pottery traditions on earth.

works.title: Mt. Fuji Tea Bowl
works.meta: Edo Japan · Hon'ami Kōetsu · early 17th c.
works.desc: A Raku-ware tea bowl with a white glazed rim called Fujisan — Mount Fuji. Kōetsu was a calligrapher, lacquer master, and sword-polisher as well. A bowl meant to be cradled.

works.title: Dabotap Pagoda
works.meta: Unified Silla · 751 · Bulguksa, Korea
works.desc: An ornate twin to the plainer Seokgatap across the courtyard. Two pagodas encoding the Lotus Sutra's doctrine that every Buddha is present in every other.

works.title: Fushimi Inari-taisha
works.meta: Heian period onward · 711 CE · Kyoto
works.desc: Ten thousand vermillion torii gates tunnel up a sacred mountain. Each is donated by a business praying for success; the writing on the back is the donor's name.

works.title: Gyeongbokgung
works.meta: Joseon dynasty · 1395 · Seoul
works.desc: The principal palace of the Joseon kings. Burned by the Japanese in 1592, rebuilt in 1867, mostly destroyed again under colonial rule — now being re-reconstructed, wing by wing.

works.title: Samguk Yusa
works.meta: Goryeo · 13th c. · Korea
works.desc: "Memorabilia of the Three Kingdoms" — a monk's compilation of legends and history. The main surviving source for Korea's founding myths and for early Korean Buddhism.

works.title: Tripitaka Koreana
works.meta: Goryeo · 1236–1251 · Haeinsa
works.desc: 81,258 woodblocks of the Buddhist canon carved to petition the gods to repel the Mongols. The blocks still print; stored in a 15th-century hall that manages its own humidity.

works.title: Streams and Mountains with a Clear Distant View
works.meta: Southern Song · Xia Gui · early 13th c.
works.desc: A long ink handscroll in the "one-corner" style — only a fraction of the silk is painted, leaving vast voids for the viewer to read as mist and distance. A founding image of Chinese minimalism.

works.title: Spring Outing of the Tang Court
works.meta: Tang Dynasty · attributed to Zhang Xuan · 8th c.
works.desc: A later copy of a lost Zhang Xuan handscroll: the imperial court rides out on a spring morning in a tight file of horses. The women in the vanguard seem to pull the viewer along.

works.title: Ink Landscape (Self-Portrait)
works.meta: Qing · Shitao · late 17th c.
works.desc: Shitao paints himself sitting on a rock, brush poised, surrounded by hills he is still deciding to render. A Ming prince turned Buddhist monk; his theoretical writings on painting are still studied.

works.title: Qiu Ying Paintings
works.meta: Ming · Qiu Ying · 1494–1552
works.desc: One of the "Four Masters of the Ming Dynasty." Rose from lacquer-worker to court painter. His handscrolls of palace scenes and mountain recluses are among the most copied in Chinese art history.

works.title: Tale of Genji Scroll
works.meta: Late Heian · early 12th c. · Japan
works.desc: The oldest illustrated version of Murasaki Shikibu's Genji. The "blown-off roof" perspective looks down into private chambers; faces are reduced to lines, clothing is everything.

works.title: Huang Quan Bird Painting
works.meta: Five Dynasties · Huang Quan · c. 950 CE
works.desc: Meticulously observed birds, insects, and turtles. Huang Quan was a palace painter in Sichuan; his "sketches from life" became the model for Northern Song nature painting.

works.title: Nishiki-e (Brocade Prints)
works.meta: Edo Japan · 1765 onward
works.desc: Multi-color woodblock prints developed by Suzuki Harunobu in 1765 — the first polychrome ukiyo-e. Enabled Utamaro, Hokusai, Hiroshige to work in full color.

works.title: Nymph of the Luo River
works.meta: Gu Kaizhi (attr.) · c. 400 CE · Song copy at the Palace Museum, Beijing
works.desc: A handscroll illustrating Cao Zhi's poem about his unrequited love for a river nymph. Figures float on waves rendered in iron-wire line, backgrounds compressed into pictographic mountains — an early statement of the Chinese approach to narrative through suggestion.

works.title: A Thousand Li of Rivers and Mountains
works.meta: Wang Ximeng · 1113 · Palace Museum, Beijing
works.desc: An 11.9-meter Song handscroll painted in mineral blue and green by a student of eighteen who died shortly after. Hardly exhibited even in China because of the fragility of its malachite and azurite pigments.

works.title: Emperor Taizong Receiving the Tibetan Envoy
works.meta: Yan Liben · 641 CE · Palace Museum, Beijing
works.desc: The Tang emperor receives Lu Dongzan, envoy of Tibetan King Songtsen Gampo, who came to arrange the royal marriage that would seal East Asian diplomacy for centuries. Yan Liben was court painter and prime minister simultaneously.

works.title: Night Revels of Han Xizai
works.meta: Gu Hongzhong · c. 970 CE · Palace Museum, Beijing
works.desc: A Southern Tang minister who suspects he is being watched throws louder and louder parties; the emperor sends Gu Hongzhong to spy, who records five scenes of the ensuing orgy. The surviving Song copy is one of the finest figural handscrolls in Chinese art.

works.title: Dwelling in the Fuchun Mountains
works.meta: Huang Gongwang · 1350 · split between Taipei and Hangzhou
works.desc: A Yuan-dynasty masterpiece painted for a Daoist friend, its 6.9-meter scroll burned in two in 1650 by a distraught collector trying to take it with him to the afterlife. The two surviving halves were reunited for one exhibition in 2011 after 360 years apart.

works.title: Shōsōin Repository
works.meta: Nara period · 756 CE · Nara, Japan
works.desc: A log-cabin-style storehouse of Tōdai-ji that has preserved nine thousand personal belongings of Emperor Shōmu — lutes, masks, glass bowls from Sasanian Persia, a rhinoceros-horn comb — under near-perfect humidity for 1,300 years.

works.title: Phoenix Hall of Byōdō-in
works.meta: Heian · 1053 · Uji, Japan
works.desc: Fujiwara no Yorimichi built the hall as an earthly image of Amida's Western Paradise, floating above a pond on narrow wings like a descending bird. Its central image of Amida is the only surviving work of the master sculptor Jōchō.

works.title: Ginkaku-ji (Silver Pavilion)
works.meta: Muromachi · 1482 · Kyoto
works.desc: Ashikaga Yoshimasa's retirement villa, intended but never actually clad in silver foil. Its ascetic gray-wood restraint and the adjacent sand garden — Moon-Viewing Platform and Sea of Silver Sand — crystallized the Higashiyama aesthetic of Japanese tea culture.

works.title: Nishi Hongan-ji
works.meta: Jōdo Shinshū · 1591 · Kyoto
works.desc: Head temple of the Jōdo Shinshū Buddhist sect. The Momoyama-period audience hall Shiroshoin is decorated with lavish painted wooden ceilings and gold-leaf panels commissioned by Toyotomi Hideyoshi — among the most opulent surviving interiors of the age.

works.title: Shugakuin Imperial Villa
works.meta: Edo period · 1659 · Kyoto
works.desc: Emperor Go-Mizunoo retired here to design tiered gardens climbing the foothills of Mount Hiei. The upper villa's dammed pond reflects borrowed scenery of the surrounding mountains — the purest surviving example of Japanese shakkei landscape design.

works.title: Chōjū-jinbutsu-giga
works.meta: late Heian–Kamakura · 12th–13th century · Kōzan-ji
works.desc: Four picture scrolls showing rabbits, monkeys, and frogs at human pastimes — sumo, archery, a Buddhist funeral. Often called Japan's first manga, the energetic ink line and biting satire of clergy are unparalleled in medieval East Asian art.

works.title: Heiji Monogatari Emaki
works.meta: Kamakura · late 13th century · MFA Boston and Tokyo National Museum
works.desc: Handscrolls illustrating the 1159 Heiji Disturbance, a coup attempt that left much of Kyoto burning. The burning-palace scroll unrolls into perhaps the greatest battle scene in Japanese art, smoke curling above warriors climbing a wheeled carriage.

works.title: Itsukushima Shrine
works.meta: Shinto · 1168 · Miyajima, Japan
works.desc: A vermilion Shinto complex on stilts in a tidal bay, with its O-torii gate standing in the water at high tide. The whole island has been considered sacred for so long that births and deaths are forbidden there.

works.title: Jongmyo Shrine
works.meta: Joseon · 1395 · Seoul
works.desc: The Confucian royal ancestral shrine of the Joseon dynasty, a 101-meter wooden hall whose austere horizontal roofline is the longest of its kind in East Asia. Twice-yearly rites performed here preserve court music banned in revolutionary China and Vietnam.

works.title: Hwaseong Fortress
works.meta: Joseon · 1796 · Suwon, Korea
works.desc: Jeongjo built this 5.7-kilometer wall to honor his executed father; the young scholar Jeong Yak-yong designed an innovative pulley-based crane to place each granite block. A complete construction manual recording every worker's wages survives from 1801.

works.title: Moon Jar
works.meta: Joseon white porcelain · 17th–18th century · Korea
works.desc: Large round jars fired in two halves and joined at the equator, allowing subtle asymmetries to soften each moon-shaped silhouette. Modern Korean painters including Kim Whanki built entire careers around the jar's quiet poise.

works.title: Buncheong Ware
works.meta: Early Joseon · 15th–16th century · Korea
works.desc: Stoneware coated in a pale slip and then incised, stamped, or brushed with freely drawn fish, birds, and peonies. Its looseness horrified the Chinese-influenced court but enchanted Japanese tea masters, who smuggled kilns full of it home in the 1590s.

works.title: Kyoto Imperial Palace
works.meta: Heian → Edo · 794–1868 · Kyoto
works.desc: The residence of the emperor of Japan from 794 until 1869. Repeatedly burned and rebuilt; the current compound follows the Heian original, walled off in parkland in central Kyoto.

works.title: Nijō Castle
works.meta: Edo Japan · 1603 · Kyoto
works.desc: Residence of the Tokugawa shoguns during visits to Kyoto. Its "nightingale" floors squeak deliberately underfoot — designed to warn of intruders. The shogunate fell within its walls in 1868.

works.title: Jogyesa
works.meta: Korean Seon Buddhist · founded 1395 · Seoul
works.desc: The head temple of the Jogye Order — the principal Korean Buddhist school. An old pagoda tree in the courtyard is 450 years old; lanterns hang from it by the thousands at Buddha's birthday.

works.title: Flying Horse of Gansu
works.meta: Eastern Han · 2nd c. CE · China
works.desc: A bronze horse galloping on a single hoof — perched atop a flying swallow. Unearthed from a Han tomb in Wuwei; so emblematic it is now the national tourism symbol of China.

works.title: Bells of Marquis Yi of Zeng
works.meta: Warring States · c. 433 BCE · China
works.desc: A rack of 65 tuned bronze bells from a duke's tomb, each capable of two distinct tones depending on where struck. A full orchestra buried in the ground — still playable after 2,400 years.

works.title: Tang Sancai
works.meta: Tang dynasty · 7th–10th c. · China
works.desc: "Three-color" lead-glazed earthenware — amber, green, cream — used for tomb figurines of camels, merchants, horses, ladies. A glittering snapshot of cosmopolitan Silk Road Chang'an.

works.title: On a Mountain Path in Spring
works.meta: Southern Song · c. 1200
works.desc: A lone scholar strolling at the bottom-left of an otherwise empty hanging — pine branch overhead, bird in flight. Ma Yuan's signature "one-corner" composition: let absence carry the scene.

works.title: Baekje Gilt-bronze Incense Burner
works.meta: Baekje · c. 600 CE · Korea
works.desc: A one-meter incense burner with a dragon base and a phoenix on top, its domed lid covered in mountains, immortals, musicians, and beasts. Pulled intact from the mud of a temple site in Buyeo in 1993.

works.title: Dogū
works.meta: Jōmon · 3rd millennium BCE · Japan
works.desc: Clay humanoid figurines with huge goggling eyes and elaborate patterning. Deliberately broken in most cases — possibly in rites to transfer illness out of the body, or funerary substitutes.

works.title: Jade Cong
works.meta: Liangzhu · c. 3300–2300 BCE · China
works.desc: Square outside, round inside — the cong was a ritual jade of the Liangzhu culture, buried with elites by the hundreds. Its cosmology is still debated, its method of drilling still not fully explained.

works.title: Sanxingdui Gold Mask
works.meta: Sanxingdui · c. 1200 BCE · Sichuan
works.desc: A beaten gold mask from a sacrificial pit unknown to any historical text. The Sanxingdui culture was erased from memory; their spectacular bronzes only rediscovered in the 1980s.

works.title: Oracle Bone
works.meta: Shang · c. 1200 BCE · China
works.desc: Ox scapulae and turtle plastrons inscribed with questions for the royal ancestors — will the harvest come? will the tooth-ache end? Cracks were heated into the bone; the answer was in the break.

works.title: Fu Hao Tomb Bronzes
works.meta: Shang · c. 1200 BCE · Anyang
works.desc: Found sealed in the grave of a Shang queen-general — 1,600 bronzes, jades, and bones. Fu Hao led armies and conducted state sacrifices, and was buried with the ritual ware of a ruler.

works.title: Funeral Banner of Lady Dai
works.meta: Western Han · c. 168 BCE · Mawangdui
works.desc: A T-shaped silk banner painted with the heavens, the earth, and the underworld, hung over the corpse of Lady Dai — whose own body was preserved so intact that forensic anthropologists have autopsied her.

works.title: Jade Burial Suit
works.meta: Western Han · 2nd c. BCE · China
works.desc: Thousands of jade plaques sewn with gold wire into a full-body armor — jade was thought to preserve the dead. The one found around Prince Liu Sheng in 1968 took 2,498 tiles and years of stitching.

works.title: Auspicious Cranes
works.meta: Northern Song · Emperor Huizong · 1112
works.desc: Twenty cranes circle the roof of the imperial palace — Huizong, an emperor who preferred painting to governing, records an auspicious omen in careful mineral pigment. He would lose the empire within fifteen years.

works.title: Wind in Pines among a Myriad Valleys
works.meta: Southern Song · Li Tang · 1124
works.desc: Li Tang's huge hanging scroll of misty pines and cliffs — a turning point in Chinese landscape, where the brush began to behave like the wind it described.

works.title: Lofty Mount Lu
works.meta: Ming · Shen Zhou · 1467
works.desc: An enormous hanging landscape painted as a 70th-birthday gift for Shen Zhou's teacher — the mountain reads as the teacher's character. A scholar's portrait in the form of geography.

works.title: Horses and Grooms (Zhao Mengfu)
works.meta: Yuan · Zhao Mengfu · early 14th c.
works.desc: Three horses and their handlers, painted in the archaic Tang manner by the leading Yuan scholar-official. Zhao turned away from Song realism toward brush-and-ink revivalism — and set the course of literati painting.

works.title: Pensive Bodhisattva
works.meta: Silla · 7th c. CE · Korea
works.desc: A gilt-bronze figure of Maitreya seated in contemplation, one leg crossed, a finger to the chin. National Treasure No. 78 — one of the subtlest sculptures of East Asia.

works.title: Moonlit Night (Shin Yun-bok)
works.meta: Joseon · 18th c. · Korea
works.desc: Two lovers beneath a willow, a narrow moon above — Shin Yun-bok's genre scenes eavesdropped on Joseon Korea's private urban life, where women and men met outside strict Confucian bounds.

works.title: Ashura of Kōfuku-ji
works.meta: Nara Japan · 734 CE
works.desc: A dry-lacquer figure of a three-faced, six-armed Ashura, somehow childlike and sorrowful rather than fierce. Cast from the imagined attendants of the Buddha, preserved in the Kōfuku-ji treasure hall.

works.title: Portrait of Minamoto no Yoritomo
works.meta: Heian-Kamakura · c. 1200 · Japan
works.desc: The founder of Japan's first samurai shogunate in black courtly robes — a yamato-e formal portrait attributed to Fujiwara no Takanobu. Possibly painted while the subject was alive, possibly a century later of a century prior.

works.title: Ban Dainagon Ekotoba
works.meta: Heian Japan · 12th c.
works.desc: A handscroll of a political conspiracy that ended in exile — a minister's slander, fire at the palace gate, panicked commoners. A masterpiece of Japanese narrative picture-telling.

works.title: Wind and Thunder Gods
works.meta: Edo Japan · Sōtatsu · c. 1640
works.desc: A pair of gold-leaf folding screens — Fūjin bagging wind, Raijin drumming thunder. Tawaraya Sōtatsu's icon of the Rinpa style, copied by Kōrin, Sakai Hōitsu, and countless descendants.

works.title: Colorful Realm of Living Beings
works.meta: Edo Japan · Itō Jakuchū · c. 1760
works.desc: Thirty silk hanging scrolls — phoenixes, roosters, peonies, carp — painted in pigments so refined that individual feathers are rendered in rows of ungleibert circles. Jakuchū gave the cycle as a meditation on impermanence to his local temple.

works.title: Takiyasha the Witch and the Skeleton Spectre
works.meta: Edo Japan · Kuniyoshi · c. 1844
works.desc: A sorceress summons a gigantic skeleton across the ruined palace of Princess Takiyasha. Kuniyoshi's triptych stretched across three sheets and into legend — Japanese woodblock at its most lurid.

works.title: One Hundred Aspects of the Moon
works.meta: Meiji Japan · Yoshitoshi · 1885–1892
works.desc: A hundred woodblock prints, each with the moon in it — Murasaki at Ishiyama, a warrior beside a ghost, a courtesan sitting up in bed. Yoshitoshi's last series, published as the West arrived and woodblock died.

works.title: Tang Dancer Figurine
works.meta: Tang China · 7th–9th c. CE
works.desc: Ceramic figures of courtly women with long sleeves mid-swing, faces carefully painted. Burial goods for wealthy tombs along the Silk Road — a snapshot of cosmopolitan Chang'an society.

works.title: The Dream of the Fisherman's Wife
works.meta: Edo Japan · Hokusai · 1814
works.desc: A woman entwined with two octopuses — the larger embracing her, the smaller kissing her mouth. Hokusai's most notorious shunga print; one of the earliest explicit pieces to influence modern Western art.

works.title: Shang Ritual Altar Set
works.meta: Shang China · late 11th c. BCE
works.desc: A complete bronze altar table with fourteen vessels arranged on it — wine containers, food bowls, ceremonial cups. Whole sets are rare; this one survived together because it was buried together.

works.title: Yu Wine Container
works.meta: Shang China · late 11th c. BCE
works.desc: A bulbous bronze wine container with swing handle, flanged with taotie monster-masks. Each piece of Shang ritual bronze is inscribed for the person it was cast to commemorate.

works.title: Ten Verses on Oxherding
works.meta: Kamakura Japan · 1278
works.desc: A Zen handscroll illustrating the parable of a man seeking an ox — mind, self, enlightenment. The text pairs Chinese calligraphy with a seated ox and a boy, finally merging.

works.title: Song of the Stone Drums
works.meta: Yuan China · Xianyu Shu · 1301
works.desc: A handscroll of calligraphy transcribing Han Yu's Tang poem — the Stone Drums, ancient inscribed boulders that schoolboys once played on. Xianyu Shu wrote it in one long, thickening brushstroke.

works.title: Old Trees, Level Distance
works.meta: Northern Song · Guo Xi · c. 1080
works.desc: A low-banked landscape: withered trees in the foreground, a misty river, mountains lost in fog. Guo Xi's essay "Lofty Record of Forests and Streams" defined the values the scroll embodies.

works.title: Goryeo Celadon Bowl
works.meta: Goryeo · early 14th c. · Korea
works.desc: Pale blue-green celadon inlaid with white and black slip — cranes in flight, chrysanthemums, clouds, rain. The Goryeo inlay technique had no equivalent in China; it is Korea's signature contribution to East Asian ceramics.

works.title: Tobatsu Bishamonten
works.meta: Heian Japan · 10th–11th c.
works.desc: A standing wooden Bishamonten — warrior of the four heavenly kings — pinning a small earth demon beneath his foot. The form, Chinese in origin, arrived in Japan via the Silk Road and stayed.

works.title: Northern Wei Buddha Maitreya
works.meta: Northern Wei · 486 CE · China
works.desc: A dated gilt-bronze Maitreya, made "in the tenth year of Taihe" (486). One of the earliest firmly dated Chinese Buddhist bronzes — the Gupta style already translated to a Chinese idiom.

works.title: A Diplomatic Mission to the Jin
works.meta: Southern Song · Yang Bangji · c. 1150s
works.desc: Song envoys crossing the Yellow River on the way to the Jurchen Jin court — a handscroll commemorating a humiliating diplomatic trip required by a treaty imposed after the northern lands were lost.

works.title: Nine-Dragon Falls
works.meta: Joseon · Han Unpyeong · late 19th c. · Korea
works.desc: A waterfall cascading in nine tiers down a mountain — Korean late-Joseon ink-and-color painting, the brush loose enough to be mistaken for the water it describes.

works.title: Snowscape with Figures
works.meta: Joseon · Kim Si · 1584 · Korea
works.desc: Bare trees, two men trudging through snow, a horse behind them. Kim Si's winter scroll — one of the finest surviving early Joseon paintings, from a period when few Korean paintings on silk survived at all.

works.title: Arrival of the Southern Barbarians
works.meta: Momoyama Japan · c. 1600
works.desc: A six-panel screen showing Portuguese ships landing at a Japanese port — exotic visitors in bell-bottomed trousers and high hats, accompanied by African attendants, disgorging Jesuits and trade goods.

works.title: Flowers and Trees of the Four Seasons
works.meta: Edo Japan · Watanabe Shikō · early 18th c.
works.desc: A six-panel folding screen of seasonal trees and flowers — cherry, maple, pine — painted in the refined Rinpa manner that came down from Sōtatsu and Kōrin into the mid Edo period.

works.title: Dragon (Muqi)
works.meta: Southern Song · Fachang Muqi · c. 1250
works.desc: A dragon coiling out of inked cloud — one of a paired scroll with Muqi's Tiger. Muqi was a Zen monk painter, much admired in Japan, where these two scrolls became standards of Zen taste.

works.title: Tiger (Muqi)
works.meta: Southern Song · Fachang Muqi · c. 1250
works.desc: A crouching tiger done in splashed ink — paw half-raised. Paired with Muqi's Dragon; the two represent complementary powers in Chan/Zen iconography.

works.title: Cloudy Mountains (Mi Youren)
works.meta: Southern Song · Mi Youren · 1130
works.desc: A handscroll of mist-wrapped peaks, executed in the "Mi dots" technique his father invented — layered wet blobs of ink building volume through atmospheric haze alone.

works.title: Landscape after Guo Zhongshu
works.meta: Qing · Bada Shanren · c. 1695
works.desc: A hanging scroll by Zhu Da, a displaced Ming prince who became an eccentric Chan monk. Bada's landscapes are spare, faintly unhinged; his "crying eyes" signature on almost every painting.

works.title: Fudō Myōō (Kaikei)
works.meta: Kamakura Japan · Kaikei · early 13th c.
works.desc: A lacquered cypress-wood Fudō Myōō with sword and rope, eyes glaring, one fang up and one down. By Kaikei, one of the paired sculptors who defined the Kamakura Buddhist style.

works.title: Flying Apsaras (Hiten)
works.meta: Heian Japan · 11th–12th c.
works.desc: A carved wood Hiten — a heavenly being playing a flute, suspended on drapery curling like smoke. Once attached as decoration to the ceiling of a Pure Land Buddhist hall.

works.title: Tanabata Inrō
works.meta: Edo Japan · Nomura Kyūkoku · early 19th c.
works.desc: A three-case lacquer inrō (small nested box) depicting the Weaver and the Herdboy — the two Tanabata star-lovers, separated by the Milky Way and reunited once a year.

works.title: Ming Chicken Cup
works.meta: Ming · Chenghua reign · c. 1465
works.desc: A small porcelain cup painted in underglaze blue and overglaze enamels — a hen and a rooster with chicks. One of the most legendarily expensive ceramic designs; a Chenghua chicken cup sold for $36M in 2014.

works.title: Eighteen Songs of a Nomad Flute
works.meta: Early Ming · 15th c. · China
works.desc: A handscroll illustrating Lady Wenji's Han-era poems — kidnapped by nomads, held 12 years beyond the Great Wall, returned south to grieve the sons she left behind. A favorite Chinese theme of exile.

works.title: Rough Waves (Kōrin)
works.meta: Edo Japan · Ogata Kōrin · c. 1705
works.desc: A two-panel folding screen of waves rendered in flat curving dark and silver — decades before Hokusai, Kōrin had stylized the ocean to a pattern of interlocked spiral claws.

works.title: Night Rain at Karasaki
works.meta: Edo Japan · Hiroshige · c. 1835
works.desc: A woodblock print of the ancient pine of Karasaki against black rain — from Hiroshige's Eight Views of Ōmi. A moonless night rendered in the brightest possible blue.

works.title: Orchids and Rocks (Yi Ha-eung)
works.meta: Joseon · Yi Ha-eung · 1888 · Korea
works.desc: A hanging scroll by the Heungseon Daewongun — regent father of the last Joseon king, painted after being forced from political power. Orchids are scholar-gentlemen; rocks are steadfastness.

works.title: Shino Tea Bowl (Bridge of the Gods)
works.meta: Momoyama Japan · late 16th c.
works.desc: A rough, irregular Shino-ware tea bowl, white feldspathic glaze dripping over iron-oxide drawings — "Bridge of the Gods." The ideal of imperfection (wabi) that Sen no Rikyū taught his student Hideyoshi to love.

works.title: Flung-Ink Landscape (Shūgetsu)
works.meta: Muromachi Japan · Shūgetsu Tōkan · c. 1510
works.desc: A splashed-ink (haboku) landscape — mountains, hut, and traveler emerging from broad inkwashes applied almost at random. The technique's restraint was the whole point.

works.title: The Bird's Nest Patriarch (Sōtatsu)
works.meta: Edo Japan · Tawaraya Sōtatsu · early 17th c.
works.desc: A Zen eccentric sits meditating in a tree, birds nesting in his hair and beard. A tarashikomi wash technique in ink — Sōtatsu's invention, which left dried puddled edges on the paper.

works.title: Winter and Summer Flowers
works.meta: Momoyama Japan · Kaihō Yūshō · c. 1600
works.desc: A pair of six-panel screens — winter branches on the left, summer vines on the right — painted with rapid black brushwork on gold leaf. Kaihō was a samurai turned painter.

works.title: Zaō Gongen
works.meta: Kamakura Japan · 13th c.
works.desc: A wooden sculpture of Zaō Gongen — a Shinto-Buddhist syncretic deity of the Kinpusen mountain. Right leg stamping the earth, sword raised, hair standing on end.

works.title: Amida Nyorai (Kōshun)
works.meta: Kamakura Japan · Kōshun · 1269
works.desc: A gilded wood Amida Buddha, the mudra of welcoming, eyes inlaid with crystal. By Kōshun, a Kamakura sculptor who inherited Unkei's workshop but pursued quieter interiors.

works.title: Prince Shōtoku at Age Two
works.meta: Kamakura Japan · early 14th c.
works.desc: A small wooden figure of Shōtoku Taishi, Japan's semi-legendary Buddhist prince, shown as a two-year-old in the traditional pose of hands clasped in prayer. Devotion in the form of an infant.

works.title: Momoyama Tea Bowl
works.meta: Momoyama Japan · c. 1600
works.desc: A thick, deliberately irregular tea bowl — raku or seto-ware, drunk from in the wabi aesthetic Sen no Rikyū articulated for Hideyoshi: the warrior as humble before the tea.

works.title: Joseon Ido Tea Bowl
works.meta: Joseon · 17th c. · Korea
works.desc: A crackled-glaze stoneware bowl, made as an everyday Korean rice bowl but valued above all others by Japanese tea masters. Ido bowls traveled east as war loot and stayed as treasure.

works.title: Plum Tree (Kōrin)
works.meta: Edo Japan · Ogata Kōrin · c. 1700
works.desc: A hanging scroll of a gnarled flowering plum — the signature Rinpa subject, picked up from Sōtatsu and passed down to Hōitsu. Kōrin abstracted the tree to its flowering gesture.

works.title: Kōetsu Calligraphy Anthology
works.meta: Edo Japan · Hon'ami Kōetsu · early 17th c.
works.desc: Calligraphy by Kōetsu over a printed background of Chinese lions and crab-finger grass — a collaboration with Sōtatsu at their Takagamine artist community. Ink and design as one gesture.

works.title: Chrysanthemums by a Stream
works.meta: Edo Japan · Ogata Kōrin · c. 1720
works.desc: A folding screen of chrysanthemums leaning over a meandering silver stream — Kōrin's signature tarashikomi wash and gold-leaf ground in one of his late masterworks.
